content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for more weird sci-fi films because I am obsessed with them

**Score:** 39
**Body:** I love weird sci-fi films that are all about humanity facing down unknowable cosmic powers and either coming out on top or going down fighting, or that use the sci-fi genre to explore complex ideas/experiences. Bonus if it's horror, lol.

Movies I've already watched: Akira (1988), Event Horizon (1997), Extinction (2018), The End of Evangelion (1997), Spaceman (2024), The Void (2016), Alien (1979), Star Trek TMP (1979), Annihilation (2018), Interstellar (2014), Iron Lung (2026), The Martian (2015), Project Hail Mary (2026)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dennis Quaid did a few—-  Enemy Mine.   Pandorum.  Innerspace.  Legion. And some more. 
2. Sunshine. Danny Boyle dir.
3. Dark City
4. A few off the top of my head that don't 100% match what you're looking for but they likely fit the "mood":

Oxygen (2021)

Meander (2020)

Aniara (2018, this one especially I think you'll dig))

Cargo (2009)
5. Sci-fi vibe + horror... somewhat recently I enjoyed The Substance. 
6. Color Out of Space


Together
7. Ex Machina haunts me. Especially lately.
8. The Day the Earth Stood Still (1951 is better)
9. Moon (2009)
10. Existenz, Primer, Gattaca, Solyent Green, A Scanner Darkly, Dark City. Paprika, Ghost in the Shell 2, Black Mirror series, Moon, Twelve Monkeys, Children of Men, isle of dogs, Donnie Darko, limitless, Manchurian Candidate, Equilibrium, District 9, Pitch Black. Demolition Man. Mickey 17, Monsters, The Lobster, The prestige, Chappie, Contact. 

## 2. Movies for a suberversive Mother's Day

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Sort of like how some people watch horror movies on Valentine's Day, I am watching Serial Mom and will likely  follow that up with Mommie Dearest.

Any suggestions that fit the vibe?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Carrie

Coraline 
2. Psycho (1960)
3. Grey Gardens 
4. We Need to Talk About Kevin
5. The Manchurian Candidate 
6. Friday the 13th. The first/original one. 
7. Mother’s Day (1980).  Come on, y’all.  It was right there.
8. Throw Momma from the Train (1987)


9. A Bad Seed
10. alien!

## 3. Positive movies!

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Movies about the greatness of humanity. Love. Caring. Inclusiveness. Family in whatever form that may take. Movies that leave you with a warm fuzzy feeling at the end. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Project Hail Mary, absolutely 
  - I loved the book! Haven’t seen the movie yet though.  
2. Little miss sunshine
3. Hunt for the Wilderpeople 
4. Amelie (2001)
5. Lars and the Real Girl
6. Roan Inish! 
7. The Peanut Butter Falcon
8. Hunt for the wilderpeople 
9. A Town Called Panic! 

Howell's Moving Castle

My Neighbour Totoro

The Song of the Sea

## 4. female obsession over another woman, platonic or romantic

**Score:** 19
**Body:** looking for media with a main or sub plot regarding female obsession and character motivations being in the name of getting attention from another woman. things like on the surface it looks like they're fighting over a man, but really deep down it's just in efforts to get closer to another woman. thinking "death becomes her", "liverleaf", "jennifer's body", "ingrid goes west". any genre is okay, bonus points if it's queer. thank you!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I suspect you'll like Single White Female 
2. Black Swan 
3. Single White Female,  The Hand That Rocks the Cradle
  - The original hand that rocks the cradle rather than the awful remake that came out last year! 
4. Heavenly Creatures. 
5. I’m not sure if this quite fits, but I’m going to recommend Rebecca (1940), directed by Alfred Hitchcock.
6. Mulholland Drive
7. Black swan, Basic instinct, The roommate 
8. All About Eve. 
9. Notes On A Scandal

## 5. I need some dark, gritty thrillers or action movies, preferably something bleak or violent.

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I'm having a bad year and need to find some emotional catharsis. I'd love to watch an action movie right about now but the usual big names like John Wick always feel way too clean. I'm a huge fan of Timo Tjahjanto's works, I'm also a big fan of the Punisher comics, especially the ones written by Garth Ennis and I think these factors are a good indicator of the tone I want.

  
I've watched Running Scared (2006) as well as Mandy (2018), both of which I also liked. Mandy especially, which is next to The Night Comes For Us as my favourite action movie. Snowpiercer seems like it would also fit the vein but I can't find it on any of the streaming services I have access to. I plan to watch it at some point.

  
I'd also prefer it if the movie doesn't have a focus on guns. I don't find gunplay interesting when it comes to movies but I'll make exceptions if it's a creative use of the weapon rather than simple firefights.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Brawl in Cell Block 99 - not just dark and gritty, but filthy (and mostly hand-to-hand fighting)
2. Green Room if you want a thriller/horror twist! But love the recs for Brawl in Cell Block 99, The Raid series, Pusher series
  - Green Room is a phenomenal thriller. I've got a high tolerance for gore, but a couple things in it made me legitimately feel lightheaded
3. Punisher Warzone is hyper violent and good fun. 

Also try Dredd, or for some more artsy violence, Nicholas Winding-Refn: Pusher trilogy, Bronson, Drive, Neon Demon, Only God Forgives, Valhalla Rising 
  - Dredd is one of the best action movies i’ve seen in a long time. 
4. You were never really here 
5. Sin City
6. The Raid
7. American Ultra. 
8. Michael Clayton - not action but it’s a suspense/thriller/drama that is definitely a gritty movie, very well written and directed. 

And yeah, check out Snowpiercer as soon as possible, it rules.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What character death messed you up for life?

**Score:** 854
**Body:** Being a huge animal lover, Sam (Samantha) from “I am Legend”. My heart broke watching that scene. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The horse in never ending story
  - Artax! Please! You’re letting the sadness of the swamps get to you.
You have to try. You have to care.
For me.
You’re my friend. I love you.”

Absolutely brutal
2. John Coffey in Green Mile
3. Sam is a G. 

One of the best dogs in cinematic history 
  - Even worse when you rewatch it knowing her end. When Will Smith frees himself from the booby trap & lands on his knife, screams in pain, & she starts licking his face to comfort him. My dog gets super attentive like that if I make any kind of sound that I’m hurting. Sam was the goodest of girls 😭
4. https://preview.redd.it/lt4ypalxdd0h1.jpeg?width=1195&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=293f7d9343add1fadfcd4870a07fe4817c9df999

The scene where the poor shoe gets dropped into acid in the film Who Framed Rodger Rabbit, I was 6 at the time and it gave me depression for life
5. ![gif](giphy|OJmCYj6KNYlSE)
6. Any dog movie.
I don't watch them anymore. 
I'm allergic to them. 😭
7. ![gif](giphy|JRsEZWr8ZqaJO)
8. Littlefoot's mom

## 2. Thoughts on Ben Stiller’s Simple Jack? 
My favorite part is where he is running through an open field, jumping in the air swinging a mallet at a butterfly. I laughed so hard I could hardly breathe

**Score:** 730

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My mom and I watch this every year on Mother's day.

It's a beautiful movie, and she says I remind her of Jack.
2. This head movie, makes my eyes rain 
  - I got gggggood brain
3. I loved that Mathew mcconaughey signed off on every call with him “say it with me , you mmm mmm mmm make me ha happy “
Fucking hilarious 
  - My favorite is when they're watching the news and the anchor goes, "But can he muh muh muh make audiences happy?"


And then McConaughey asks, "Did you see the news? Like pistol whipping a blind kid."
    - McConaughey had so many gems. I almost pissed myself when he says, "At least you get to choose yours" as he stares at his kids picture
4. I feel I want to say that this movie resonates with me the same way as Sean Penn's My name is Sam, but I can't quite put my finger on why.
  - the fact that you got the title of the movie wrong makes it even funnier.
5. Don’t see many movies in theaters these days but if they actually made Simple Jack I’d drag my ass there.
  - That would ma-ma-ma-ma-make me happy 

## 3. I’ll tell you what I’d do, man.

**Score:** 713
**Body:** Starship Troopers (1997)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Two citizens at the same time
  - I did my part!
    - Service guarantees citizenship!
  - Do those look like the kind of citizens that would double up on a civilian like you? 
2. Amy Smart is still fine af.  Aging like fine wine.
  - TCW (Tasty Coma Wife) for the win.
3. PETER! CHECK OUT CHANNEL NINE ITS THE BUG EXAM
4. ![gif](giphy|14g6PIAY8f6FeU)
5. “What would you do?”

“ besides two chicks at the same time?”

“ well yeah…” (as if he’s answering with the dumbest question of all time)
6. I did not realize the other girl was Amy Smart.

## 4. A movie whose world and characters deserve further exploration through sequels or series. My pick would be Dungeons & Dragons: Honour Among Thieves

**Score:** 651

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh I get to be the person that says “The Nice Guys!”
2. Dredd with Karl Urban. 
3. I heard they are making a tabletop game out of this movie
4. It’s absolutely   


https://preview.redd.it/6lv7blnokc0h1.jpeg?width=584&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=07acd27c38928b720ad11da2f8f0c8b20d4e3bac
5. The Black Woman in the court scenes is THE FUNNIEST OUT OF NOWHERE CHARACTER In the movie !
  - They're throwing potatoes!
  - Oh Jarnathan! 
6. not exactly this but that valerian and the city of 1000 planets or whatever it was called had one of the coolest universes I've ever seen and everything other than the main plot was 100x more interesting than the main characters 
7. Ive seen this mentioned elsewhere and I feel like its a great idea. Keep the same cast but have everyone playing a different role in the next film with an entirely unrelated plot. Then it would really be like a new DnD campaign with the same players as different characters 
8. It was so good!  I didn't expect to love it

## 5. What movie do you consider a perfect movie?

**Score:** 251
**Body:** For me it's 12 Angry Men. Everything about it was perfectly done in my eyes.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

2. My Cousin Vinny

The quality of the sequals can be argued.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Penda’s Fen (1974) is fantastic

**Score:** 23
**Body:** "I am mud and flame”

Penda’s Fen is above all a film about miss fitting: a boy who believes he lives within a solid order discovers, little by little, that this order was religious and cultural fiction. Stephen begins as a disciplined young man, aligned with conservative Christianity, militarized masculinity, and a rigid idea of ​​England; but the film peels away each of these layers through visions and revelations that force him to reconsider his own body and origins. It is in this sense of dismantling that the film finds its strength: it is not just a coming-of-age drama, but a coming-of-crisis drama

Sexuality is the first major vector of this crisis, but the film treats it indirectly and in a profoundly unsettling way. Stephen does not “discover” his sexuality as one receives stable information; he is traversed by desires that disorganize his morality and his imagination. Through nightmares and visions of angels and demons the he finds an subjectivity that no longer fits into the categories he has learned to repeat and impose upon himself

This same logic applies to cultural heritage, especially in the clash between Christianity and paganism. The film does not simply oppose "good" and "evil"; it suggests that institutional Christianity was a historical layer imposed on an older England. Therefore, paganism emerges less as exoticism and more as local memory, which remains beneath the surface of the landscape and language

The film insists that knowledge is not born neutral: it is shaped by institutions, names, discourses, and by "official versions" of history. The English landscape is treated as an archive, and the names themselves function as indexes of historical layers that the dominant culture attempts to organize into a continuous narrative. The film dismantles this continuity by showing that England is a territory of impositions, not of essences: a space where language, religion, and identity have been rewritten by conquests, intersections, and repressions

As a film Penda's Fen impresses by transforming internal conflict into broad cultural critique. The result is a drama that is both lyrical and political, in which sexuality, religion, language, and memory do not appear as separate themes, but as parts of the same process of dismantling what seemed natural

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. Mysterious Skin and how almost every scene represents a part of sexual trauma

**Score:** 15
**Body:** A lot of people dismiss Mysterious Skin as shock value because of how direct and uncomfortable it is. But personally, I think almost every disturbing or sexual scene in the film serves a psychological purpose connected to trauma, grooming, dissociation, hypersexuality, and vulnerability.

The movie does not explain these themes directly. Instead, it communicates them through behavior, short interactions, body language, and repeated sexual situations. If you remove those scenes or skip through them because they are uncomfortable, I think the emotional and psychological core of the film collapses.

A lot of Neil’s prostitution scenes are often criticized as excessive or unnecessary, but I think they are essential to understanding his trauma.

Neil does not simply “like sex” or enjoy prostitution in a shallow sense. The film repeatedly shows how his abuse distorted his understanding of affection, validation, power, and intimacy. Because of the grooming, he associates sexuality with attention, value, and emotional connection.

Many of the clients reinforce the illusion that Neil now has control over his sexuality and body: Some let him “lead” the encounter. Some appear passive. Some seem emotionally distant.

This creates the feeling that Neil has transformed his trauma into something voluntary and empowering. But the film undermines that illusion.

For example, after one encounter, Neil suddenly begins hyperventilating when memories of the abuse resurface. Later, he shows Wendy the bruises left on his body, destroying any glamorous or empowering reading of his lifestyle.

The scenes are not framed as triumphs, they are tied to anxiety, dissociation, emptiness, and emotional instability.

If these scenes were removed, the later violent assault would lose much of its meaning. Without the buildup, it would just look like another shocking scene. But with the previous encounters in mind, the assault becomes the destruction of Neil’s belief that he had regained control over his trauma. 

The film shows that Neil’s confidence is partly a survival mechanism, an attempt to reinterpret abuse as agency.

One of the most disturbing scenes is when Neil, as a child, imitates abusive behavior toward another child.

I do not think this scene exists for shock alone. I think it represents how grooming can completely distort a child’s understanding of intimacy and sexuality. Neil interprets what happened to him as something connected to reward, affection, and pleasure because he was manipulated into seeing it that way.

The scene demonstrates how abuse can spread psychologically into future behavior and identity, especially when a child has no healthy framework to understand what happened. And since they are not always perfect and sad victims, but can also cause harm, especially if they are young children 

However, if you don't consider the context behind it, that scene becomes just a disgusting scene that seems unnecessary.

Brian represents a completely different trauma response.

While Neil internalizes the abuse into his sexuality and identity, Brian psychologically escapes from it through dissociation and fantasy. His alien abduction beliefs function as a protective structure that allows him to avoid consciously processing the abuse.

This is why the scenes involving the UFO show woman matter narratively.

When the older woman validates Brian’s beliefs and emotionally supports him, Brian begins to feel safe and understood. But when she suddenly crosses sexual boundaries, the illusion collapses. It's like he's reliving the grooming given by the coach all over again. 

I think this scene parallels Neil’s storyline. Both characters believe they have found stability or safety, and both discover they remain deeply vulnerable because of unresolved trauma.

The scene also demonstrates how survivors who have not processed abuse can become vulnerable to manipulative or exploitative dynamics again, even in completely different contexts.

The final revelation completely recontextualizes the film.

Up until that point, Neil still partially frames the abuse through ideas of affection, intimacy, or being “special.” But the final memory destroys any possibility of romanticizing what happened.

The abuse is revealed as something deeply invasive, humiliating, and psychologically incomprehensible for children. I think the extremity of the final revelation is intentional because it explains why both protagonists became so psychologically fractured.

Brian dissociates entirely from reality and Neil transforms the abuse into hypersexuality and emotional confusion.

I think Mysterious Skin can absolutely feel overwhelming, uncomfortable, or even unbearable. I understand why many people reject it emotionally.

But personally, I do not think the disturbing scenes are there simply to provoke the audience.

If you only watch the surface events, the film can look like “constant sex and violence.” But if you follow the psychological progression of Neil and Brian, almost every scene contributes to the same central idea, how childhood sexual abuse reshapes identity, memory, intimacy, and the way people try to survive emotionally afterward.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are people who think it's all gratuitous shock value?  It's literally taught in film theory classes.  That's how I first saw it.

The shower scene with the steroid guy was absolutely bonkers.  The Johnson and Johnson shampoo bottle (no more tears).  It's not subtle in that particular moment, but it all builds up to the finale and the question of what Neil's truth actually is.
  - I tried watching it with someone who considers himself a "cinephile," and who has seen many films, and he didn't want to finish it because he thought it was shock value, and that I was weird for trying to analyze it. 

The truth is that the first time I saw it I was alone, and I also found it shocking, but after thinking about it and analyzing it, after seeing it a second time, I was able to draw all the conclusions in the post. 
  - Echoes the breakfast cereals and toys and such, no?
2. Well explained.

I relate to ways both characters cope with their trauma, and your analysis seems spot on to me, and accurately reflects my experiences, as well.

Seems right. 

And I guess this doesn't hit the length requirement, so....

...can you recommend any other films exploring people coping with trauma in a grounded artistic/creative way?
  - I am a CSA survivor myself, so as I watched the film I began to understand what each scene and part of the film might be trying to represent. As you say, it reflects my own experiences too, but I still needed a second look to organize my thoughts.

To answer your question, unfortunately no, I don't know many films that deal with trauma in general, let alone sexual trauma. A few months ago I saw Silenced, but that film deals more with sexual abuse from a perspective of corruption, and it's a drama that attempts to discuss how we react as a society when systematic abuse is perpetrated by beloved figures of power. If you're interested in that approach, you'll find it good too. 
3. Very well said! I feel weird saying I like this film considering the content, but I do find it to be one of the most harrowing and realistic depictions of trauma and think it does an incredible job tackling a sensitive topic that most films wouldn’t dare touch. 

## 3. Apex (2026) | Not Bad, Not Great

**Score:** 15
**Body:** A serviceable action thriller held together mostly by its beautiful landscapes and (I assume) real locations. As a survival thriller it’s not bad but it could’ve been great. It’s not as immersive as it should’ve been and I think there are two reasons. 

The first is the impossible CGI assisted camera work which really takes you out of the world. The camera follows characters down water falls and into the water and then up in a seamless single take or go from swimming with its subjects in rapids to pulling out to a wide shot. 

I think when showing fantastical events (physically possible or not) the camera should be treated like it’s shooting something real. The coverage should feel like it’s covering a real stunt. A large reason why something like Pacific Rim works is because of that.

Also it’s hard to buy Charlize Theron as someone in danger from most threats. She comes across like a real life Amazon from Themyscira. Taron Egerton    
is great as the villain but I never once thought Theron couldn’t just fold him. 

There is a scene late in the film where Theron speaks about the tragedy that opens the film. Egerton’s psychotic serial killer almost makes a connection with her in that moment. They’re both tied to each other deep in the jungle, far from civilisation. It’s followed by a scene where they have to climb a cliff together and Egerton has to completely trust her.

I thought that whole scene showed the film’s wasted potential. That’s such a good sequence on paper and the actors really sell it but the rest of the film doesn’t have the foundation to support it. 

Still, it’s not a bad film. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You see the movie ha sgood actors decent plot and production YET it just feels like another netflix movie. I don't seme to put my finger but I don't know what's wrong with such flicks . They just don't seems exciting. I used to love such genre of movies back from 90s and pre 2010. However now a days they just don't click. 
  - Predictable. 
2. Agreed. It’s serviceable. There’s potential but most of it is wasted on a contrived silly plot and the lack of a sense of real danger; which is fundamental to the entire film working as intended. 

The beginning is trying so hard to repeat Cliffhanger and it just lands so flat. The fake CGI set extensions and unreal floating camerawork  completely negate any sense of danger or life and death stakes. 

The two main leads are endlessly watchable but the script gives them nothing to do but run after each other. The few dialogue scenes they share later in the film feel like they might have somewhere interesting to go but alas it’s all just filler to add a few minutes of runtime.

The best thing about the picture is it reminded me immediately of Cliffhanger, which is now of the rewatch list. That one’s still got my favorite Lithgow quote:

“Do you know what true love is? Sacrifice.”
  - Yeah, there seemed some bizarre discrepancy between the great acting and the action plot, or in general some heavy dissonance in where they were trying to go with this.


Egerton would have worked great if he did, like, actual animal/survivalist stuff: You, know lurk in the dark, run around naked, covered in mud, climb trees, sew up his flesh wounds and reset his bones, set up nasty traps, drink his own piss, etc, etc... I feel thats what they wanted to present with his dialogue and background, but then what he actually does is just run around really fast with his 3000 dollar crossbow.


I really dont get it, there are endless of these "most dangerous game" jungle-survivalist thrillers, as well as RL survivalist know-how, that all give you endless inspiration for how to pull this off effectively, but they seemed to have completely ignored all of that tradition.


The setting also seemed to, from the start, go against setting this up well. If they are in some reservate 30 miles from civilisation that doesnt set you up well for a nice survivalist thriller. You want them to be in the remote wilderness where its not about running faster but these kinds of gnarly survivalist skills.


You slightly wonder if thats supposed to be the point, is he meant to be just some middle class guy with narcissistic delusions about being the animal apex predator? But no, too much stuff that goes against that too, and in fact he is all kinds of super human, just not in a way that feels slightly credible. Not an animal, just some middle class guy who runs at 70mph for some reason.


Really sad for Egerton, if they had given the action-plot some proper substance he might have been rightfully hailed immensely for his acting.

## 4. Truly absurdist/surrealist films

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I watched 2 Luis Bunuel/Salvador Dali films: Un Chien andalou and Lage Dor

One thing I noticed is, compared to some surrealist films I’ve seen today, these truly don’t have an interpretation.

It even borders on the line of comedy, a recurring gag of kicking animals/blind people into the stratosphere, or a woman nonchalantly leading a cow from her bedroom. 

I feel like I haven’t seen this sort of energy in a film before. It’s truly meaningless and there’s no point in even deriving an interpretation from any of it.

Even in Lynch films, I can only think of Inland Empire that’s truly “absurdist”, but there’s still general themes and interpretations that are commonly held in his works.

But are there any true modern absurdist works that are just insane and don’t even care about meaning anything? 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I can honestly say REFLECTIONS OF EVIL (2002) by Damon Packard is the most absurd movie, you won't believe it got made. Unique, one of a kind, hilarious, brutally difficult on the viewer. 
  - Oh my God someone else remembers this movie! It's totally insane and one of the best things ever. Highly recommended. 
  - I couldn’t remember where I knew the name Damon Packard from…..looked it up and for some reason I watched Foxfur at one point! Completely unhinged but an interesting watch nonetheless
2. The only one which comes to mind is Dream Journal 2016-2019 by Jon Rafman.

Its a mixture of surrealism and internet irony. I haven't watched in a while, neither do I know the 2 works you mention, so I don't know if it suits your criteria.

What I liked most about it is that I could tune in at any point and admire the ideas and their execution, and not have my experience rely on following a narrative or prioritizing logic, wouldn't make much of a difference anyway. So my own memory of it is as scattered and vague as a dream. In comparison my memory of conventional films which incorporate surrealism is still based on the same elements as any other genre. How I remember Mulholland Dr is closer to how I remember Chinatown than how I remember Dream Journal/ 

As for Lynch I recall him specifying that he was more into using "dream logic" rather than the assumptions people have of him.
3. Neco Z Alenky by Jan Svankmajer springs to mind. The Czech mannerist surrealist created a stop motion version of Alice in Wonderland, complete with a taxidermy white rabbit, animal bones and odd dream like symbolism dashed in for good measure. There is an American dubbed version, but really the original works as the dialogue is more just an explanation of what is on scene, so having it in Czech doesn't detract from the experience. 


Holy Mountain should also be mentioned, but you need to watch that going in blind. 
4. I would suggest the anthology movie FUNKY FOREST (2005). It's possible there were some sort of message hidden somewhere, but it's not one I could sense in any shape or form. When I watched it, my brain pretty early gave up on applying meaning to any of it. It was sort of an exhausting watch, but I made it through, even with the intermission. Loved Notti's Dream, though; nice music!

I also remember some pretty transgressive danish movies from the sixties or so. Basically porn, but aiming for being as disgusting as possible. No clue what they were called.

In a long-gone cinema, I saw what I think was called "smil", by (I think) the Cobra movement. It was an unbroken close-up shot of a smiling female mouth, lasting for ten minutes. No sound, except from the anxious half-laughter which was sometimes heard from the audience. Finally, it ended. Only to be replaced with a close-up of an eye. At that, we were screaming with laughter.
5. There are countless international experimental film festivals that screen works that may come close to what you are looking for. However, having said that and in my opinion, it is a weird time for such works to exist now, here’s hoping for a new wave!

## 5. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (May 10, 2026)

**Score:** 11
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **The Dark Knight (2008, Christopher Nolan) — re-watch** — Not sure how much I have to add to what I said about this movie last time, except that I feel like the Nolan trilogy’s (deserved) reputation for being smarter and darker than other superhero movies has somewhat overshadowed just what a blast they are. Still a must-watch. **9/10**

I’ve decided to take on another series as a block (well, most of one). This time, **the John Wick movies (except the first one):**

**John Wick: Chapter 2 (2017, Chad Stahelski)** — I feel like I’m the only one who doesn’t get the love for these movies. I put off watching *Chapter 2* for a long time because I didn’t really care for the first one, but I’ve finally given in, and I find them both to be… fine. *Chapter 2* is at least more different in tone from the first one than I expected; where the first *John Wick* didn’t aim to be much more than a straightforward revenge story, *Chapter 2* attempts to add a bit more of a contemplative and philosophical element into the action. While that could, in theory, be more interesting, the execution is, if anything, even more basic and tediously predictable than in the original. I imagine that most of this movie’s viewers just came to watch cool fights, and it does manage to have plenty of those, so, in that sense, it succeeds. As a complete movie, not so much. **5/10**

**John Wick: Chapter 3 — Parabellum (2019, Chad Stahelski)** — Still not seeing it.

**START OF SPOILERS**

 I think my biggest issue with these sequels is thatI don’t really *get* the High Table, like, as a concept. They’re some kind of shadowy organization that makes the rules for the criminal underworld… but for some reason, their rules permit, and, in some cases, even require those working under them to assassinate their own members? I guess one *could* say that nobody thinks it would even be possible to kill a Table member, but we already saw John do it twice in the last movie, and it didn’t seem to be *that* difficult, at least for an elite-tier assassin like Wick. Given that it’s supposed to be a millennia-old organization, it seems implausible that nobody has ever done it before.

**END OF SPOILERS**

Narratively and stylistically, *Parabellum* is… basically just more of the same things we saw in the previous two movies. I guess I should say that at least the fight choreography is still strong, but, honestly, even the fights are so repetitive at this point that I can’t bring myself to enjoy almost any of them. The only thing that seems to be new now is that Stahelski has just made the characters’ injuries more frequent and more graphic — to the point that Wick not dying from them by the end now reads less as a sign of how tough he is, and more a sign of how lazy the writers have gotten.

Actually, that’s not entirely true. There is at least one other notable difference between *Parabellum* and the first two movies, which is the complete abandonment of the idea that criminal conspiracies are supposed to be secretive. In the first two movies, the gangsters weren’t exactly going to great lengths to hide what they were doing, but there were at least *occasional* nods to the fact that things like this are supposed to be done covertly; when murders or attempted murders happened in public places where civilians could use it, it was presented as something unusual enough to cause people to panic. Here, they are apparently just able to kill people anywhere, at any time, and nobody bats an eye. It’s another way in which the whole thing just feels dumber and sillier than the previous movies did.

Like I said, I didn’t really love either of the first two movies, but I think this is the first movie in the quadrilogy that I actively disliked. Would not recommend. **4/10**.

**John Wick: Chapter 4 (2023, Chad Stahelski)** — Well, it’s less of a waste of time than *Chapter 3*, at least; the action is more fun and creative, and the story actually resolves something, instead of just being an entire movie of padding where everything of consequence is undone by the end. On the other hand, the action still feels a lot dumber than in the first two movies — by the time it got to the point where John cut a man’s neck by throwing a playing card, it was starting to feel less like an action movie and more like an extended episode of *Happy Tree Friends* — and the series still continues to confuse having characters repeat slight variations on “John isn’t the sort of man who will ever give up violence” over and over again for having deep themes. Also, is it just me, or is Keanu giving off major “I’m so bored of having to play this character” vibes here? I guess it *could* be an intentional choice to convey Wick’s exhaustion with the life he’s in… but I doubt it. I continue to take the contrarian position that the first *John Wick* is the only good one, and that even that’s pushing it. **5/10**

**Movie of the week:** *The Dark Knight*
2. **Robot Dreams (2023, Pablo Berger)**  
I adored this animated film. It shows the friendship between Dog and Robot and the journeys they go through together and separate. Cute on the surface, but some deeper messaging behind it. Great music too, with Earth Wind and Fire! **8.5/10**

**The Golden Glove (2019, Faith Akin)**  
This one was super grisly and disturbing. Based on a true story, it portrays the life of Fritz Honka, a German serial killer from the early 70’s. Pretty depressing watch. **6.5/10**

**Hokum (2026, Damian McCarthy)**  
Highly anticipated, watched it in theaters and it blew me away. Might be movie of the year for me so far. Adam Scott is perfect as the role of Ohm Bauman, a writer who has suffered through trauma and loss and has become an absolute asshole as an adult. He visits a haunted inn in Ireland where everything starts spiraling from there. The scenes in the honeymoon suite were incredibly creepy, the jump scares were really well done (donkey man and ghost of his mom). I loved Fiona, she made a big impact with minimal screen time. I really loved this film and can’t wait to watch it again to pick up details that I missed. I had a hard time sleeping that night and that’s saying a lot. **9/10**

**Send Help (2026, Sam Raimi)**  
This was a fun watch! The dynamic between office dork, Linda Little (played by Rachel McAdams) and hotshot CEO, Bradley Preston (played by Dylan O’Brien) was great! The only survivors of a plane crash, Castaway but dark comedy. Lots of laughs to be had with this one. **7/10**
3. Chirin's Bell (1978): a classic anime that had been on my list for years, but which I'd avoided as it seemed too childish and silly to actually watch. Until three days ago, I knew only that it was "about a lamb who wears a bell." Turns out it's dark, deeply deeply dark, kind of an anime Bambi and beyond, as this playful carefree lamb experiences grief and decides to become ... a death machine. So it's about transformation and loss -- not just of loved ones but also of a sense of belonging, self and home. This beautifully drawn cartoon is *not* for little kids.

Jet Storm (1959): A man aboard an intercontinental flight recognizes a fellow passenger as the hit-and-run driver who killed his daughter and evaded punishment. As the grieving father's plan to avenge the crime by destroying the plane midflight (this isn't a spoiler; it's basically the starting point) leaks to the crew then to everyone else on board, we are treated to amazing insights about human nature. Excellently acted characters include a bitter widow (Hermoine Baddeley) and the grieving father (Richard Attenborough). It's intense and feels remarkably authentic.
4. I just saw Bodies, Bodies, Bodies and found it to be a fun take on the whodunnit genre. Horror comedies are a favorite of mine so I might be biased. I found the dialogue hilarious and the actresses' work very solid. 

On the other hand, Death. A neo noir thriller with a gay lead. The cinematography is beautiful, the plot twists are very interesting and the side plots, engaging. I recommend this one for neo noir aficionados.

Bulbbul. A bollywood horror movie. Its better if you go in without knowing too much but it's a vampire story with a lot of nudges to the original Dracula. 
5. The best movies I saw were revisiting Fargo, revisiting Miller's Crossing, and watching The Seven Psychopaths. 

The worst movies I saw this week were probably The Boss and Catch and Release. 

I saw an Adam Sandler film (Just Go With It) which wasn't very good, but which led me to Cactus Flower, with Walter Mathau and Goldie Hawn (who won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actress for it). 
6. **If I Had Legs I'd Kick You** (2025) \*\*\*\* A mother's life collapses around her. This was more surreal than I was expecting, esp. the way the camera is intently focused on Rose Byrne like she is being examined under a microscope, which is why it's no surprise she was nominated for an Oscar. There's a dark humor to it all, and oddly Conan O'Brien is the least funny character in the cast. I have no idea what the title means.

**Lover, Beloved** (2022) \*\*\* A one woman musical about the writer Carson McCullers that was written and performed by Suzanne Vega. Apparently she's been working on it for years, and I was familiar with a few of the songs. It was intriguing at first, but it's a little too esoteric for someone who knows nothing about McCullers. What I did get out of it is that Vega is an excellent actor with a theatre degree, and it's nice to have this context for some of her music. She could easily go the way of Laurie Anderson if she wanted.

**Piaffe** (2022) \* An erotic German film about a novice sound designer who studies horse sounds so intently for a pharma commercial that she grows a tail. The photography is lovely but it's light on eroticism and arty without being compelling. 

**Written on Water** (2016) \* Hour-long documentary about water rights disputes over the Ogallala aquifer in Texas that doesn't present the information very well or explain the conflict. All I learned by the end is that they're running out of water. 

**Rambo** (2008) \*\*\*\* I went in with low expectations but this turned out to be a solid and sober action/war film. The story is simple, but Stallone directs the heck out of it, and the violence is incredibly brutal (in the vein of *Private Ryan*). It occasionally gets histrionic, but for the most part it's about a man who is haunted by knowing how much of a lethal killing machine he can be. I don't think anybody wanted a Rambo movie in 2008, but it's the best since *First Blood*. 

**Troy** (2004) \*\*\*\* I heard the director's cut was an improvement over the original, but it's been so long since I saw the original that I can't compare. *Troy* is a good movie that never becomes the great movie it needs to be to rise to its legendary subject matter. I'm not sure what's lacking. The effects are great, the production values are sumptuous (if a little too clean), the battles are decent (and apparently gorier in the director's cut), the actors are physically magnificent (with Brad Pitt in stunningly peak shape) and the hair is beautiful. However, the acting is ham-fisted, as they usually are in these kind of epics, to the point of laughter sometimes, and there's little depth to the story. I guess Wolfgang Petersen couldn't muster the nuance the story needed.

**The Apprenticeship of Duddy Kravitz** (1973) \*\*\* Fresh out of high school, Duddy is hell-bent on getting rich and schemes and hustles to the point of alienating those around him. Will he develop empathy or will he grow up to be Gordon Gecko? Richard Dreyfuss is terrific in this little Canadian film he made between mega-hits *American Graffiti* and *Jaws*. The film is adapted from a novel and you can tell by the way it skips around a multitude of characters and plotlines, which makes it feel extremely long and rambling. If it were a tighter film it could have been a classic. The supporting cast is great, esp. Denholm Elliott. 

**Cactus Flower** (1969) \*\*\* A mild comedy with 60s sensibilities that, like *Vivacious Lady* I watched a few weeks ago, is all built around a strained premise: Walter Matthau doesn't want a serious relationship, so he tells his girlfriend he's married with kids. Once you swallow that plot pill, it's down to the performers to carry the show. Goldie Hawn won supporting actress in her first film role for being absolutely adorable, and she avoids being the ditzy blonde, but the movie really belongs to Ingrid Bergman in her first American film in decades. Bergman's Scandinavian ice queen is filled with repressed desire and it's great to see her blossom. Matthau is completely miscast as a philandering ladies man with a 21-year-old lover, but he underplays and handles the role the best he can. Someone like Tony Curtis would have been a much better choice. Apparently Lauren Bacall played the role for two years on Broadway and was upset she wasn't cast in the film. 

**The Hallelujah Trail** (1965) \*\* Several groups clash over a wagon train shipment of whiskey. Admittedly, I dozed off through a lot of this film, but it's a cartoony Western comedy that simply isn't funny. It's unsophisticated and witless humor aimed at 8-year-olds. The best thing I can say about it is the restoration looks great and the mountain scenery is beautiful. Maybe it should have been animated?
7. **Judgment at Nuremberg (1961, dir. Stanley Kramer)**

Appropriately epic in scope, Judgment at Nuremberg takes its time in covering the tail end of the Nuremberg Trials that sorted out the dregs of National Socialism in the years immediately following Allied victory in Germany. There's no catharsis to be found in Judgment at Nuremberg; with Hitler, Goebbels, and Goring dead, and so many other high-ranking Nazis tried or MIA, it's hard for these characters to get very excited about closing the book on an ugly chapter in history. Germany and the US both want to move on, one looking to celebrate who's survived the war and the other looking for a new enemy. The film tackles rich themes of nationality, culpability, and the ephemeral, objective qualities of justice. It doesn't give any easy answers, and while its characters do make definitive statements and stances, there's still enough room for the viewer to come to their own conclusions about topics we're still debating. The performances compliment this handsomely. Spencer Tracy is careful, world-weary, but good at heart and stubbornly principled; Burt Lancaster toes the line perfectly between sympathy and pity, commanding the scene almost entirely through his sullen body language and downcast facial expressions until he gives one of the best monologues in cinema; and Maximilian Schell is a true firebrand, standing by his ideas and defending them until his howitzer-adjacent voice gives out. The court scenes are more exciting than most action scenes from more conventional WWII movies, and that's precisely because of the acting and the writing. Well worth a watch for anyone interested in the intersection between bodies of oppression and ordinary people.

**Sold for Marriage (1916, dir. Christy Cabanne)**

Would make for a decent horror thriller a la Fargo season five - a young Russian woman is kidnapped to the States by ruthless family members who want to sell her to a potential husband. Unfortunately, subject to poor direction and a bad script, Sold for Marriage is a limp drama that feels more like a vehicle for sexism and xenophobia instead of a meaningful statement about the exploitation of women. It doesn't help that the storytelling is very confusing and plainly surreal. Why was there a magic storm in the main character's village ahead of her travel to the US?

**Resident Evil: Degeneration (2008, dir. Makoto Kamiya)**

More consistent and palatable than the live-action Resident Evil movies, but less entertaining than later animated features from the franchise. The animation is, understandably, pretty lackluster, but the writing is unengaging and feels more appropriate for a video game than a movie. It doesn't help that the plot is fairly convoluted and even though there are only three or four characters doing anything at any given time, it's still easy for one or two to get lost.

**Zodiac (2007, dir. David Fincher)**

A thrilling, creepy crime drama. Fincher's direction is supremely unsettling, and he does a great job of building up the paranoia and tension. I was pretty unfamiliar with the Zodiac Killer ahead of watching this, and I was surprised to learn that his reign of terror had lasted so long. All I had really known about Zodiac was that he was never caught; however, that doesn't mean that his identity is a mystery. The film makes a meticulous, compelling argument for one suspect, and within the realm of the film you'd be hard pressed to make a counterargument. I really like the film's structure, focusing at first on the failure of the police and then the ambition of Jake Gyllenhaal's investigative journalist character, Robert Greysmith, who would go on to write the book on which Zodiac is based. I've seen countless movies based on nonfiction books, and I don't think I've ever seen this sort of metatextual plot swerve. It was pretty cool, and made an interesting argument about the concept of justice. The police in Zodiac failed to catch the killer for a lot of reasons, but while legally his identity will likely forever remain obscure, that doesn't mean that reality has to be hidden behind layers of bureaucratic red tape. Greysmith doesn't give up the fight, and now a private citizen can make a cohesive, objective argument against another private citizen, one corroborated by circumstantial evidence and eyewitness testimony. Now I've never read Greysmith's book, so his argument could be total bupkis, but I think the idea of finding nonviolent justice outside of flawed, old guard institutions like the police is worth exploring, and Fincher does a beautiful job.
  - 
**Where Are My Children? (1916, dir. Phillips Smalley, Lois Weber)**

Unafraid to tackle sensitive social topics, Smalley and Weber's ambition and passion are commendable. Shoes is heartbreaking, and Dumb Girl of Portici is vivifying. They could have done beautiful things with a movie about abortion and eugenics, but Where Are My Children is sadly flawed. The husband-and-wife directing duo take a staunch pro-life stance, framing abortion as a uniquely selfish act that women commit to subvert their unassuming husbands and their dreams of building a family. Accompanying this is commentary on the need to abort the children of the extremely poor so as to clean the community's gene pool. The commentary on eugenics is underdeveloped and, frankly, strange and confusing. The main character's husband is a eugenicist who wants to use the pseudoscience to gestate a perfect child, but this goes nowhere thematically or narratively. As much as I dislike the social politics of Where Are My Children?, I do appreciate how brazen Smalley and Weber are. State and city censor boards could be pretty persnickety in 1916, so it's surprising that they could get away with so much open talk about abortion and eugenics. They probably broke barriers in this way, setting a precedent for more intelligent stories later on. That doesn't excuse bad writing, but I think Smalley and especially Weber deserve to be praised as pioneers. The visual style of the film, utilizing lots of double exposure and fantastical imagery, is also commendable, as well as many of the performances, namely Tyrone Power Sr. as the jilted husband.

**Civil War (2024, dir. Alex Garland)**

This film's reputation precedes it. I didn't expect a firm political stance, and I was pleasantly surprised by just how deep and nuanced the film's commentary is. While it doesn't directly address the rift between liberals and conservatives in the US, it still reflects our sociopolitical reality. The war journalists are stand-ins for common Americans; like them, we sit on the sidelines of major political developments, observing and passing off relevant information but never acting or getting directly involved. All the while, we internalize the trauma we witness, filing it away for a later use that will never concretely or meaningfully arise. Garland revives old criticisms of the neutrality of photojournalism, framing his characters as indifferent soldiers of fortune in a decaying world, therefore subtly criticizing our own inaction in less violent but still turbulent times. I'd have to rewatch Civil War to really determine if Garland makes a concrete argument after making this comparison, but I really appreciate the ambivalence of this film, forcing the audience to come to their own conclusions. Perhaps watching this two years later, when you know just how bad it can get, has contextualized my opinion and mindset watching the film. I certainly felt more fired-up in the climax than I would have in 2024.

**Memories of Murder (2003, dir. Bong Joon Ho)**

Watching these one day apart from another, I feel similarly towards Memories of Murder as I do towards Zodiac: broadly, both are about the inherent limitations of the police and their lackluster response to an "unsolvable" crime. But where Zodiac highlights a silver lining in Robert Greysmith's exposure of the Zodiac Killer's true identity, Memories has no hope, focusing more on the cost of obsession and the consequences of brutal, unjust violence. The police can identify forensic trends in this serial killer's spree, but that brings them no closer to actually identifying and prosecuting the true murderer. There's something, multiple somethings really, preventing the detectives from finding them. It's so much easier to torture confessions out of the mentally-disabled and arrest people because of the color of their underwear. It's grim, but never unsatisfying and always captivating.

What really stands out to me in Memories of Murder are the character arcs. The two central protagonists are Park Doo-man, a local detective, and Seo Tae-yoon, a detective visiting from Seoul. As a Twin Peaks fan, I was expecting Seo to bring in cognitive and forensic resources otherwise totally unavailable to this boondocks town and really coordinate the investigation, but Seo is no Agent Cooper. Likewise, Park is no Sherriff Truman. While he doesn't become a good person by the end, Park comes to recognize the futility of his actions and just what this kind of big-stick policing was costing. He ends the film on a relatively mellow note, while Seo goes in the opposite direction. He starts out ordinary, but he revelas himself as a bottom-line kind of guy, not batting an eye at torture and coercion until a contradiction in someone's testimony emerges. He grows more impatient and debases himself with more immoral actions as the film goes on, falling as Park rises. It's a really compelling dynamic, and takes the film to dark, ambiguous places. It's what takes Memories of Murder from great noir to exceptional drama.

**The World and the Woman (1916, dir. Frank Lloyd, Eugene Moore)**

Rudderless until it decides to become a movie about faith healing. The movie is content to be a hangout-type story until the main character suddenly demonstrates the ability to heal people's wounds magically. Themes of faith and redemption are brought up, but not sufficiently explored. It doesn't help that, while the main character is a prostitute, the intertitles can never explicitly mention her sex work, which becomes problematic when a character uses this fact as the lynchpin in a moralistic argument levied against her. While the story is muddled at best, the photography is lovely and Jeanne Eagels' performance is very nice.

---
